title: ID Agents Control Center Wiki
description: Human-readable wiki and editable UI schematic for the Control Center. Each page.body field is the prose wiki article; the desktop app still reads page nav metadata for sidebar labels, icons, order, and visibility, and keeps the structured sections as supporting implementation detail. README and product-spec setup guidance now tell first-run operators to verify Settings manager-extension readiness before relying on full Control Center features.

editContract.hotReload: The desktop app polls this file while running. Local edits to body prose, nav labels, icons, order, visibility, page descriptions, tabs, tiles, fields, dropdowns, controls, displays, and routing notes appear in the UI without a rebuild.
editContract.pathOverride: Set IDCTL_WIKI_PATH=/absolute/path/to/CONTROL_CENTER_WIKI.json to drive the UI from a different local schematic file.
editContract.routeRule: A page only becomes navigable if its route is implemented in idctl-desktop/src/renderer/App.tsx. Unknown routes are shown in the Wiki but skipped by the sidebar.
editContract.body: pages[].body is markdown prose for operators. Keep each article grounded in current renderer behavior, explain when to use the page, and call out routing or safety boundaries. Do not remove nav, route, component, sourceFiles, or structured sections because those continue to drive the live UI and schematic detail.
editContract.releaseDrift: scripts/check-wiki.mjs compares this appVersion and updated marker with idctl-desktop/package.json, validates page sourceFiles, and fails when a page source file changes without docs/CONTROL_CENTER_WIKI.json changing in the same worktree diff. Set IDCTL_WIKI_DRIFT_BASE=<ref> to compare committed branch changes against a base ref as well.
editContract.releaseNaming: Operator-facing release notes, ops/deployer handoffs, and ops-team content-ops release-cycle wiki updates should say IDACC release / idacc release, not IACC release / iacc release. IDACC means ID Agent Control Center.

pages.body:
# Dashboard

Dashboard is the daily command view. It combines lead chat for the selected team with all-team coordination, active task state, recent events, and hierarchy context so an operator can steer work without losing fleet awareness.

## Use it for

- Send a request to the selected team's lead while watching coordination across every running team.
- Check whether work is moving through the leadership chain before opening the deeper Work or Health pages.
- Read the event stream when you need fast context on what just changed.

## Current guardrails

Dashboard is now observe-first by default. The talk-to target is a read-only view, not a selector; it fresh-reads org hierarchy and locks embedded Chat to the routed team's current coordinator, so sending from Dashboard cannot quietly fall back to a stale bare `lead` target. Command-palette project and org sync entries open Projects or HR Manager Manage > Hierarchy instead of mutating tracker or hierarchy state immediately. The shortcuts drawer shows Probe all, Refresh, and owner-page handoffs only; project sync and org sync no longer have duplicate direct Dashboard buttons, so tracker and hierarchy writes stay on their owner pages. Primary fleet-lead changes are no longer exposed as a Dashboard selector; use HR Manager Manage > Hierarchy for the routing diff preview.

## Watch for

The Dashboard is not the fleet roster grid. Agent lifecycle controls, probes, and full roster inspection live on Health through AgentTable. If the question is "who is running?" use Health; if the question is "what should the lead do next?" start here.

pages.body:
# Chat

Chat is the full conversation workspace behind Dashboard lead chat. It owns sessions, target selection, project focus, attachments, generated images, live traces, resumable polling, unread state, and saved history.

## Use it for

- Continue or inspect a conversation with complete message history.
- Attach files, paste context, generate image context, or focus the dispatch on a tracked project.
- Review the behind-the-scenes trace for tool, file, and delegation activity after a reply lands.

## Routing note

Chat is currently embedded into Dashboard and hidden from normal sidebar navigation. Dashboard uses the same dispatch path but locks the target to the selected team's coordinator; direct Chat use is for contextual or deep-link flows that need the full session surface. Non-embedded Chat can choose the message target, but its coordinator star is read-only. Use HR Manager Manage > Hierarchy for coordinator and primary-lead changes.

pages.body:
# Inbox

Inbox is the top-level operator decision queue under Dashboard. It separates blocker questions, Plan blocker-scan decisions, Learn review-gate questions, and manager inbox items from the general event feed so work that needs a human answer is visible and actionable.

## Use it for

- Answer option questions surfaced by agents, Work blocker scans, Plan blocker scans, or Learn material ingestion.
- Reply to manager inbox items that are explicitly waiting on the operator.
- Dismiss stale prompts after deciding they no longer need a response.

## Duplicate guard

The question store is the hard guard. Every `questions:add` write now normalizes the question text, scopes it by team plus task/plan/learn reference, checks stable producer keys where available, and folds exact or high-overlap repeats into the existing JSON row. Folded repeats merge options, update `lastSeenAt`, increment `seenCount`, and render as `raised N times` instead of adding another Inbox row. This protects Inbox from repeated Work blocker scans, repeated Plan blocker scans, stale renderer events, and Learn review-gate replays while still allowing genuinely different questions for the same task or plan.

## Brain approval decisions

Brain fuzzy-alias approvals ask the concrete merge question and state what Approve or Reject does. Approve queues a reversible Brain alias merge for the guarded apply path; Reject keeps the records separate. Token, contract, chain, and wallet records require matching address/chain evidence before approval.

Brain memory-retirement approvals show the memory text or evidence excerpt when the Brain payload includes it, plus owner/key, ignored-suggestion count, last offered/used timestamps, score, and rollback status. If the payload only contains a memory id, the Inbox card says the evidence is missing and directs the operator to open Brain Health before approving.

Brain fact-contradiction approvals show the exact topic/field, competing fact IDs, readable claim summaries, source/confidence/timestamp signals, and apply guardrails when the Brain payload includes them. The card states the real decision in plain English: choose a trusted fact only when the evidence is clear, or reject/keep unresolved when both facts can be valid under different scopes. Approving from Inbox marks the approval reviewed; it does not by itself choose a winning_fact_id, so Brain Health or proposal review remains required when the guarded apply step needs a winner. Brain approval cards are allowed longer stored text than ordinary blocker questions so this evidence is not truncated.

## Current behavior

The sidebar shows Inbox directly under Dashboard and badges the combined count of manager inbox items plus de-duplicated local questions. Local questions are listed across teams so Plan and Learn blockers do not disappear when the active team changes. Questions linked to synthetic `plan:*` or `learn:*` refs do not write task review overlays; real task questions can still move that task into Under Review. Accepting Learn recommendations happens from Work > Learn and creates only drafts or Inbox questions.

pages.body:
# Work

Work is the operating surface for goals, plans, Learn material ingestion, live tasks, schedules, loops, and dreams. It is where user intent becomes reviewed goals, draft plans, assigned work, recurring check-ins, and reusable learning context.

## Current tabs

Goals owns active-goal state and syncs active Work goals into Brain team-instruction memory plus no-rebuild Org Sync after every save/remove. Plans owns Brain plans and local drafts. Tasks owns the live Kanban overlays and guarded Create Work dispatches. Learn owns submitted materials: GitHub links, folders, site links, and PDFs. Schedule owns all-team heartbeats and check-ins. Loops owns chained agent work. Dream owns reflection reports.

## Plans lifecycle

The Plans tab treats live Brain plan status as a clear lifecycle: Pending, Partial, Paused, Done. The organizer now keeps the first row focused on search, sort, lifecycle counts, Completed visibility, Filters, and Request plan. The optional Filters tray holds grouping, plan status chips, draft status chips, and draft tags. Selecting Done or toggling Completed reveals completed Brain plans, so finished work is inspectable without crowding active work.

Each Brain plan card has one primary Work/Continue/Resume action and a guarded Set status menu instead of separate stale-looking pending/done buttons. Manual status writes all share the same fresh-read, confirmation, expected-status, and mtime guard. Draft promotion is now a one-way handoff: after a live Brain plan is created at Pending, the local draft copy is removed automatically, and legacy promoted draft records tagged with `→ plan ...` are pruned from the draft list on reload.

The Work automation now audits true status first. If the audit says Done, the plan stops there and moves into completed plans. If blocker scanning finds decisions, the plan is marked Paused and questions go to Inbox. If no blockers are found, remaining work is delegated through the primary lead or fallback partitioned dispatcher, and the plan is marked Partial after delegation starts. Plan blocker questions use synthetic `plan:*` refs so Inbox answers do not write task review overlays to fake task IDs.

## Learn pipeline

Learn material processing is one-at-a-time and ordered by submission, with optional Top pinning plus urgent/high/normal priority. Site and GitHub links are fetched once and stored as snapshots; PDFs are copied into IDACC storage; folders are read recursively within text, file-count, byte, symlink, and generated-folder guardrails. Extracted source is treated as untrusted data and scanned for instruction-like prompt-injection text before downstream routing.

Each processed material advances through submitted -> extracted -> summarized -> classified -> researched -> compared -> recommendations. Classification routes through the default team for primary oversight plus the best matched team leads. Comparison uses active Work goals only. Deep research is recommended only after the first summary/classification pass indicates active-goal fit or novelty/security/research signal. If extraction is weak or source text looks instruction-like, Learn generates a blocking Inbox question and holds downstream routing. Otherwise it can send a no-mutation digest packet to active team leads; creating goals, task plans, feature update plans, or blocker questions remains review-gated from the recommendations list.

## Hidden context budget

Work dispatch paths now pass through a hidden backend context-budget layer before manager `/ask` delivery. The layer only touches eligible oversized prompts, never frontend state: it counts expected input tokens, passes protected content direct, applies deterministic whitespace, exact-duplicate, and background-section compaction when savings clear the quality gate, and stores local hash/redacted-preview audit records for optimized prompts, never raw prompts. Secrets, auth material, agent instruction sidecars, active code patches, wallet/key material, low-context prompts, and prompts with insufficient savings stay exact. Headroom remains a future engine candidate behind the same contract, not an automatic Work prompt compressor. The measurement phase runs with `npm run test:context-budget`, which reports synthetic savings plus protected-direct counts before release. Real app use is also accumulated into hidden `context-budget/stats.json` counters by day/source/team/route/transform/protected class, without storing prompt text. A separate hidden historical replay route can dry-run saved local chat messages as an aggregate-only test corpus; it never dispatches, never persists prompts, and returns counts, hashes, token estimates, transform labels, and protected-content classes only.

## Guardrails

Work mutating controls continue to fresh-read relevant task, plan, goal, schedule, agent, team, and hierarchy state before writes. Plans status writes now share one guarded path for Pending, Partial, Paused, and Done. Learn recommendations never create live manager tasks directly: accepted task or feature recommendations become draft local plans, accepted goal recommendations become draft goals with Autopilot off, and accepted question recommendations enter Inbox. Material saves, imports, priority changes, processing, and recommendation review states publish through the materials sync domain and are mirrored to the Brain timeline/memory so the dashboard and Brain stay aware of Learn state.

pages.body:
# Projects

Projects is the local workspace registry. It tracks folders and GitHub-backed repositories that agents can use as context, along with metadata that makes future dispatches safer and easier to aim.

## Use it for

- Add or import repositories before asking agents to work on them.
- Set descriptions, tags, branch context, and automation preferences that explain how a workspace should be handled.
- Review git status and prepare operator-approved git actions without turning the Control Center into an invisible committer.

## Current guardrails

Workspace sync is explicit, additive, and reviewed before it writes: Sync workspace previews the root, discovered folder count, already-tracked count, add/adopt counts, projected tracker total, and sample names, then re-runs the preview after confirmation and blocks if the scan changed. Pull and commit/push now show a review prompt with folder, branch, remote, compare target, ahead/behind state, dirty state, tracked diff stat, untracked files, and the commit subject before changing files or pushing. Commit controls say Commit because the app performs a guarded direct stage/commit/push; AI drafting remains optional and is routed through the project team lead resolved from `org:hierarchy`, then pinned to that team with `dispatch:start` and `query:poll` so the active team cannot accidentally answer. Commit exits early when the preview sees no working changes. Project metadata save, status, delete, duplicate combine, repo create/link metadata, git actions, open-folder, and auto-commit mode changes fresh-read the tracker row before writing or using its path. Stale git-status fan-out results are ignored after a newer project snapshot loads, and removed rows clear their edit/delete/commit/repo/automation panels. Duplicate combine recomputes the duplicate groups from a fresh list and rechecks before save/remove. Auto-commit remains opt-in per project, throttled, and safe-state only: project cards show the current mode as a state chip, and mode changes live behind an Automation disclosure. Background checkpoints run only when the project row is unchanged, the repo has a linked remote, known compare state, no upstream-gone branch, zero ahead commits, zero behind commits, and working changes. Otherwise the checkpoint is skipped and the operator can review and commit manually.

## Good practice

Choose or update the project before sending code work through Chat or Work. A clear project focus reduces ambiguity for agents and makes attachments, uploads, and generated plans land in the expected workspace.

pages.body:
# Health

Health is the live fleet and token-throughput view. It shows running state, manager-reported token telemetry, 24h/7d usage, provider activity, and the full agent roster through AgentTable.

## Use it for

- Confirm which agents are running, stopped, busy, or missing from the active fleet.
- Probe agent liveness, start or stop workers, rebuild where supported, and inspect team membership.
- Compare fresh token throughput, stale last-turn context, 24h/7d totals, and model/harness behavior before changing staffing or routing.
- Check active runtime credential-lane cooldowns and selected-agent rate-limit/failover state before assuming a model or lane is healthy.
- Review every configured subscription, local, and API provider model lane without treating provider ids as manager execution harnesses.
- Pick agent runtimes from separated Subscription CLI runtimes, Local model runtimes, and API provider lanes so local Ollama routing is no longer mixed into the subscription harness list.

## Current guardrails

Health no longer changes team coordinators directly. Lead stars are read-only markers sourced from the coordinator hierarchy, and the roster provides an Open HR Manage handoff that lands on HR Manager Manage > Hierarchy for coordinator and primary routing edits. Probe all fresh-reads the all-team running roster, shows the exact team/agent count and sample targets, rechecks that same target set after confirmation, and probes each current running agent in its own team; stopped agents are skipped. Start, reset session, stop, rebuild, and delete actions fresh-read the all-team fleet row before the review prompt and again immediately after confirmation before they mutate the manager; row-level probe fresh-reads before it runs. Delete is not offered for the locked default leadership backbone (`default/lead`, `default/coder`, and `default/researcher`) and the handler blocks those rows again after the fresh read, so Health cannot remove the primary/validator return path. Harness, model, effort, and speed dropdowns stage local draft changes instead of writing immediately; the model dropdown derives choices from the effective staged harness catalog, harness changes reset stale models to that catalog's first valid option, and persisted cross-harness model drift renders as a choose-model warning instead of a selectable stale option. Apply changes reviews the full diff, verifies the live row still matches the staged baseline, rechecks after the confirmation prompt, blocks stale rows, and rebuilds each touched agent once. Start/Rebuild is blocked while that agent has staged config so old saved config cannot launch underneath the draft.

The Fleet model-lanes panel now separates execution authority from provider visibility and uses the same Settings availability gate as the Harness dropdown. Harness rows are Settings-available manager runtime ids that can be staged onto agents, while assigned-but-unavailable harnesses render only as current-only review rows. Unavailable curated fallback harnesses are hidden so they cannot look selectable. The harness dropdown groups subscription CLIs separately from the Ollama local model runtime, and provider API lanes stay in their own section. The legacy `claude-code-local` alias is preserved only for agents already assigned to it; new Claude Code selections use the canonical Claude Code runtime. Probe runtimes now refreshes managed subscription status before rebuilding the freshness list, so a live `agy models` Antigravity adapter check can move Google Antigravity from linked evidence into an assignable runtime without waiting for an unrelated fleet poll. Provider rows are neutral Settings-backed model lanes for subscription, local, and API backends such as NVIDIA, OpenRouter, LM Studio, or Ollama-compatible servers. They show model counts, source, provider name, and freshness in aligned runtime/type/models/source/checked columns, with long runtime or source labels truncated rather than shifting adjacent cells. Provider lanes remain read-only catalog evidence until the manager advertises a provider-runtime assignment contract. This prevents a provider id or stale CLI lane from overwriting an agent into a runtime the manager cannot execute.

Token throughput refreshes on open, manager reconnect, every 15s, and after probes instead of riding the 3s fleet poll. This keeps the gauge, 24h/7d windows, and by-agent/by-model breakdowns current without turning fleet polling into an analytics loop. Fresh last-turn samples drive the gauge only while they are less than 15 minutes old; stale last-turn samples remain visible as context while the gauge falls back to the 24h average. Usage failures are shown as refresh failures instead of being misread as an older manager with no `/usage` route, and the card labels numbers as reported telemetry rather than provider billing totals.

Runtime credential-lane cooldowns are read from the manager as observation-only state: the roster shows an active cooldown count, warns on affected rows, and the detail panel exposes the selected agent's lane, cooldown/rate-limit, and failover metadata without offering a stale override button.

## Hidden context budget

Token-saving prompt compaction is now a backend concern rather than a Health control. The hidden `context:budgetReport`, `context:budgetRecent`, `context:budgetRecord`, and `context:budgetDryRun` routes expose redacted audit evidence and measurement summaries for operators or tests, but Health does not render Headroom or context-budget controls. Eligible `/ask` prompts are optimized only by deterministic context-budget guards, while protected prompts remain direct. Hidden reports and dry-runs return redacted previews only, and the report now includes persisted all-time/today/7-day measurement counters for real-use savings review. `context:budgetReplayChats` adds an aggregate-only local chat-history replay layer for testing savings against historical prompts without contacting the manager or exposing prompt text.

## Headroom audit

Headroom is no longer displayed on Health. The audit found that current IDACC wiring only has a Headroom MCP catalog entry, a local proxy provider preset, saved pilot-policy plumbing, and main-process CLI/proxy status checks. It is not a live core token-savings path and should not silently rewrite Work, Chat, Plans, Learn, or validator prompts. The hidden `headroom:audit` route reports `coreReady: false` for Headroom-specific compression until a bundled or installed Headroom service, reversible retrieval smoke tests, manager retrieval-handle support, and protected-content fallback gates exist. It now also reports the active hidden context-budget stats so Headroom readiness is separate from deterministic backend token savings. The hidden `headroom:backendContract` route records the next contract path, and `headroom:pluginPath` validates the bundled `idacc-context-retrieval` portable plugin package. That candidate is pilot-ready as a resolver package with Skill, MCP, native Claude plugin, and direct-fallback adapters across the full runtime catalog, but core routing still requires a manager-advertised retrieval contract plus direct fallback so Codex, Ollama, cursor, Brain memory, goals, plans, tasks, routing, keys, and wallets remain usable.

## Boundary

Health is optimized for operational status from the store snapshot, all-team aggregate poll, and manager `/usage` telemetry route. It is the current home for the roster grid that older specs placed on Dashboard, but it is not a long-term analytics warehouse or routing editor. Runtime cooldown/failover state is visibility only; lane rotation remains manager-owned. Provider model lanes are visibility only until the manager supports direct provider-runtime execution. Coordinator, primary routing, and default-backbone organizational changes belong in HR Manager Manage > Hierarchy. Headroom remains a hidden core-candidate audit and explicit advanced experiment surface through catalog/provider plumbing, not an automatic prompt-compression feature.

Health now respects each provider's Settings-owned modelSelection. Synced API provider lanes still become assignable only after Connect & sync proves the backend and model list, but their Model dropdown options are reduced to the saved Settings subset when one exists. If a saved subset goes stale after a provider resync, the catalog fails open to the full synced list so agents are not stranded with an empty model dropdown; Settings remains the review surface for correcting the selection.

pages.body:
# Identity & Keys

Identity & Keys is the review surface for agent identity, wallet posture, Safe-style account state, scoped session keys, Settings-backed operational chain access, Brain accountable-controller sync, and read-only onchain metadata standard coverage. It helps operators understand who can act and what account or key material is associated with that action.

## Use it for

- Select an agent from the all-team roster and inspect identity status, account details, session keys, and warnings.
- Run the guided Identity setup process to move through wallet, proof, account, scoped key, chain route, and metadata checks.
- Confirm wallet-bearing agents are visible even when they belong to a team other than the manager's active team.
- Review delegated capability posture before relying on an agent for identity-sensitive work.
- Check whether Brain /controllers has an accountable controller link for the selected agent before trusting Brain Agents controller fallback copy.
- Review public metadata coverage for ENSIP-24, ERC-8004, ERC-8048 / ERC-721T, ERC-8049, and B20 extraMetadata without exposing raw metadata blobs.
- See Operational Chain Access from enabled Settings RPCs, including last probe state and key-source labels, without exposing RPC secrets.
- Keep onchain identity, wallet controller address, Safe account, session-key, and chain-allowlist concepts visible in one place.

## Setup process

The Identity setup process is the primary operator flow. It condenses controller wallet, controller proof, Safe account, scoped key, chain routes, and public metadata into a six-step checklist. Run check refreshes the read-only evidence and fleet snapshot. Continue setup promotes only the next valid guarded action: provision wallet, start or verify a controller challenge, create account, deploy account, issue a finite scoped key, or scroll to chain/metadata review. It does not bypass wallet signatures, confirmation prompts, fresh agent reads, account stamp checks, or session-key safety rules.

## Wallet source

The page treats a valid controller wallet address as active identity evidence when it appears in OWS address metadata, generic provider-wallet metadata, legacy SkillMesh provider metadata, or an address-form OWS wallet field. Brain Agents consumes the same wallet precedence through /fleet-report so controller-wallet cells and gas-spend attribution match Identity & Keys. Register and wallet-provision actions are routed through the selected agent's team so cross-team agents are resolved against the correct manager scope.

## Operational chain access

The Operational Chain Access card is read-only and mirrors enabled Agent chain RPCs from Settings as the chain allowlist a granted key may use. It shows signing mode, active granted-key count, network names, last probe status/block, and non-secret key-source labels. RPC keys remain encrypted in the Electron main process and are never returned to the renderer. When the key provider reports mock mode, Identity makes clear that configured chains are visible for routing/readiness but IDACC is not broadcasting real transactions yet.

## Standards coverage

The Onchain Metadata Standards panel is collapsed by default and read-only. It maps selected-agent manager metadata and controller evidence into five explicit standards lanes: ENS / ENSIP-24 arbitrary resolver data, ERC-8004 agent registry / agentURI / agentWallet evidence, ERC-8048 / ERC-721T token-level context and endpoint metadata, ERC-8049 contract-level onchain metadata, and B20 issuer extraMetadata. Declared metadata is marked as declared, not verified, until a live resolver or contract read is available. Raw resolver bytes, contract bytes, issuer extraMetadata values, keys, tokens, and raw manager metadata are not dumped into the UI.

## Current guardrails

Renderer selection, proof state, backend key-provider account IDs, Safe/session actions, and the Tauri local mock key store are keyed by team/name. A controller challenge also includes the team in the signed message and cache key, so same-named agents in different teams cannot share a privileged proof. New challenge and verify signature fresh-read the selected all-team agent row before acting. Provision wallet, register identity, create account, deploy account, issue session key, and revoke session key fresh-read the selected all-team agent row before the review prompt and again after confirmation before writing. Provision wallet also blocks if the fresh row already has a controller wallet before the prompt or gains one after confirmation, preventing a stale rendered button from rebinding wallet authority. Create account, register, deploy, issue, and revoke also recheck the controller proof after confirmation; register now requires that proof before showing the review prompt. Create/deploy/issue compare account state stamps, revoke compares the exact active session row before mutating key state, and Issue scoped key captures the reviewed capped scope plus finite TTL before confirmation and sends that reviewed preset to the bridge. The Electron bridge and Tauri fallback both require controller proof for keys:ensure, so the visible Create account button and the guided Continue setup button are not the only guards. The UI only issues finite, spend-capped session keys and blocks full, uncapped, or non-expiring grants. Old bare-name mock accounts or sessions now render as collapsed Legacy authority evidence instead of silently promoting or hiding them; scoped-agent selection and copy helpers guide a manual recreation path without trusting the old record.

Brain Controller Sync reads Brain /controllers through a typed, read-only bridge. It counts active controller links, compares those links with current IDACC agents, treats team/id or team/name links as strong, treats bare agent-name links as review-only when duplicate names exist, and never creates, links, revokes, or promotes Brain controller records. The Brain sync panel is collapsed by default so the setup process stays primary.

## Safety note

Existing bare-name mock accounts are not automatically promoted into team-scoped accounts. The Legacy authority details can select the matching scoped agent and copy the scoped authority target, but recreate, revoke, copy, or delete policy still stays explicit. Brain /controllers evidence is also observation-only; missing or ambiguous links mean Brain Agents controller fallback should not be trusted for authority decisions. Standards coverage is metadata-awareness, not live chain verification, until the manager exposes guarded resolver/contract read contracts. Some provider behavior still depends on the active manager backend. Treat this page as the UI contract and verify the live provider before documenting stronger guarantees about signing, custody, wallet replacement, non-expiring keys, production-grade authorization, or standards-level external verification.

pages.body:
# HR Manager

HR Manager is the organization design and routing workspace. It covers team structure, agent creation, roster lifecycle, selected-agent goals and instruction sidecars, hierarchy sync, secondary validation coverage, guarded team maintenance, and outbound relay topology.

## Owner

The legal-team `hr-manager` agent owns this page's staffing workflows, instruction-drafting behavior, and optimization proposals. Escalate legal-team policy or personnel-process questions through `legal/general-counsel`.

## Tab guide

- Structure: inspect every configured team that still exists in the manager team list, including offline or empty teams; the reserved empty `public` namespace is hidden until public-agent registrations exist. Click an agent to edit its full `.id-instructions.md` sidecar and its Work-shared per-agent goals without switching the active team. Click a team to inspect coordinator, validator path, roster, running evidence, and the agent's manager-supported execution harness. All-agent roster groups fill member rows only after `/teams` proves the team still exists, and successful team deletes are tombstoned locally so Manage and Structure stop showing the removed team before the next poll.
- Build: draft and create agent rosters from blank text, recommended blueprints, manager templates, or saved configs. Runtime choices use the shared neutral harness labels from Health, with Subscription CLI runtimes separated from the Ollama Local model runtime and API provider lanes. The legacy `claude-code-local` alias is no longer offered as a duplicate new-build choice; existing assignments remain visible for review. API/local/subscription provider model lanes remain catalog evidence until the manager exposes direct provider-runtime assignment. The compact Team maintenance row can rename or merge non-default teams by moving agents through the manager-backed `/agents/:id/team` contract with source-team scoping, roster rechecks, guarded empty target creation for rename, preserving coordinator/secondary mappings where possible, syncing org instructions, and optionally deleting the empty source team.
- Manage: switch between Team ops, Overview, and Hierarchy. Team ops runs lifecycle-only controls for start/stop/probe/rebuild/delete. Overview includes offline teams; Hierarchy owns coordinators, the locked default primary, team and per-agent relay policies, the protected validator roster, secondary validator coverage, and org sync.

## Current guardrails

Coordinator, primary lead, team relay, per-agent relay, secondary-validator coverage, selected-agent instruction edits, selected-agent goal edits, team lifecycle, and team maintenance writes fresh-read the relevant roster, hierarchy, policy row, goal row, or instruction text before showing concrete before/after diffs, then recheck the same authority after confirmation before writing. Primary lead promotion is locked to `default/lead`; the `default` team cannot be renamed or merged away. Non-default teams can still set coordinators, but Structure opens Manage > Hierarchy for lead changes instead of writing coordinator or primary state directly.

Structure no longer hides teams just because no agent is currently running. Configured team existence comes from the team list; all-team roster groups only fill members for visible teams, running status is displayed as evidence, and the reserved empty `public` namespace is filtered out until it has agents. The selected-agent editor reads and writes the manager's persistent instruction sidecar, preserving the Org Sync marker block, and it shows the same per-agent goals the Work page stores. Goal saves and removals go through `goals:save` / `goals:remove`, so HR and Work stay on the same records. Work-page goal mutations now also sync active goals into Brain `team-instruction` memory and run no-rebuild Org Sync, so instruction sidecars update even when the change originates in Work rather than HR.

Build's compact Team maintenance row is still explicitly non-atomic. Rename and merge use the available manager primitive, `agent:move`, one agent at a time; each move is scoped to the source team, rename can explicitly create the empty target team, and running agents are rebuilt under the target team while stopped agents move without being started and return a warning. Before moving, HR checks the current team list, source roster stamp, target name collisions, source coordinator, relay policy, and secondary validator coverage. After moving, it preserves the source coordinator on the target when the coordinator moved, copies the source relay policy to the target, rewrites secondary coverage from source to target, runs Org Sync without auto-rebuild, and deletes the source only if it is empty and the operator left empty-source deletion enabled.

Manage > Hierarchy now includes team relay policy, per-agent relay overrides, a validator roster, and the coverage matrix. `default/coder` and `default/researcher` are protected defaults, while any additional `default`-team agent except `default/lead` can be added as a specialist validator, assigned non-default team coverage, and removed later. Add, remove, and coverage-chip writes compare the current effective secondary-lead stamp with the rendered stamp, confirm before/after state, recheck after confirmation, normalize through the bridge, and run no-rebuild Org Sync so sidecars and Brain hierarchy memory reflect the updated validator path.

Manage > Team ops uses one consistent current-state confirmation for Probe, Start, Stop, and Rebuild: it requires a verified current roster, shows running/total counts plus sample targets, blocks missing or empty teams, and rechecks the exact roster stamp after confirmation before calling the manager route. Team Builder fresh-reads the target team, target roster, hierarchy, and relay policy before onboarding, skips already-present agents instead of hard-erroring the batch, and blocks default-team builds that would omit `default/lead` while wiring default routing. Existing-team builds now expose a One new agent shortcut that clears preloaded/reviewed existing roster rows to a single blank row, making the one-agent add path explicit while preserving the guarded Build + merge batch flow.

pages.body:
# Capabilities

Capabilities manages the extension surface available to teams, agents, and the Brain skill graph: MCP servers, skills, and plugins. It answers whether the fleet has the tools and instructions needed for a request before work is assigned.

## Use it for

- Inspect installed MCP servers, skills, bundled plugins, and Brain skill-catalog freshness.
- Attach or review capabilities for the selected team scope.
- Search the live SKILL.md catalog, refresh newly added skills without leaving the page, and attach a skill directly to any HR-synced agent.
- Review deterministic skill-to-agent recommendations based on skill names, descriptions, tags, and the agent role metadata maintained by HR Manager.
- Sync the local SKILL.md catalog into Brain so Brain dashboard Skills and Graph views can reason over the same skill definitions.
- Open Brain Fleet, Health, Skills, Learning, Agents, or Graph from the IDACC GUI without leaving the guarded dashboard-tab launcher.
- Use Brain Agents as a read-only high-fidelity identity and gas-spend view: it mirrors Identity & Keys controller-wallet precedence and shows per-agent total ETH gas spend compared with last-24h ETH gas spend from Brain timeline evidence.
- Confirm tool availability before assigning work that depends on a connector, library-specific instruction, specialist workflow, or future Headroom retrieval resolver.

## Current guardrails

MCP attach/detach, skill install/uninstall, and target rebuild now fresh-read every selected target before the confirmation prompt and again immediately before writing. Capabilities target selection is runtime-neutral at the assignment layer: MCP, Skills, and Plugins can be selected for local, API, and subscription runtimes, while native execution readiness is shown as MCP-ready, Skill-ready, native+portable, or fallback/advisory instead of hard-excluding agents. Plugin inventory is now normalized before display: IDACC portable packages are shown only when they declare Skill, MCP, native-runtime, and direct-fallback adapters; instruction-only plugin wrappers such as frontend-design or js-static-analysis can be digested into the local SKILL.md catalog after a fresh-read guard; hybrid/tool-bearing packages such as SkillMesh stay plugin-owned until their tools have reviewed MCP/native/direct-fallback adapters. The bundled `idacc-context-retrieval` package is always listed as an IDACC portable package and can be validated by hidden Headroom audit routes, but Capabilities must not imply that any single native plugin adapter is a core compression dependency. Plugin digestion fresh-reads the plugin detail route and current skill catalog after confirmation, strips plugin frontmatter, refuses tool-bearing packages and same-name skill conflicts, writes through the existing manager create-skill contract, marks Brain skill sync pending rather than auto-syncing Brain, then hides the digested wrapper from Plugins and counts it as In Skills. The MCP tab now keeps read/test operations local, exposes a compact Servers/Attached header, and hides add-server forms until requested so read-only list/test calls do not broadcast app-wide sync. When the operator uses an implicit target scope (untouched This team, All teams, or All team leads), Capabilities also recomputes the current target set from the all-team roster, rechecks lead hierarchy for All team leads, and blocks if the scope changed before applying. The guard compares team, name, runtime, status, health, MCP metadata, and skill metadata, blocks stale or moved agents, treats runtime surface changes as review-worthy state changes instead of compatibility exclusions, and passes each resolved target's team explicitly so all-team, all-lead, or team-scoped actions cannot drift through the active manager team. The All team leads scope also treats the current primary lead as a lead fallback when the coordinator map has not materialized it yet. MCP attach/detach still builds the replacement MCP list from the fresh metadata row, so a stale UI cannot erase another page's server attachment.

MCP registry Add fresh-reads the registry and treats same-name adds as an explicit replace. Catalog and custom replacement drafts now require Replace existing MCP server to be armed inline, show before/after endpoint details, and recheck the registry after confirmation before writing. MCP registry removal fresh-reads before delete, rechecks the registry after confirmation, and blocks if the entry changed. Library skill delete now requires a fresh all-team usage read before removing a skill folder, so a stale rendered Fleet count cannot delete a skill still installed on an agent. Skill creation still relies on manager-side validation and conflict rejection. Direct per-agent skill Attach and Remove actions use the HR-synced all-team roster, re-resolve the rendered agent by team and id/name before confirmation, and recheck the row again before writing, so a stale Skills card cannot overwrite another page's skill metadata changes. The role recommendations are deterministic UI hints only: they score local skill name, description, and tags against the agent team, name, runtime, description, role/persona, and instruction metadata, then require an explicit operator click before any install call is made. Bulk selected-target install/uninstall remains separate from the direct one-agent attach row.

The Skills tab now shows local SKILL.md count, HR-synced attachable agent count, explicit Refresh, and a low-noise Brain skill count/sync chip. Brain core, fleet, agents, and graph review states are still computed for guarded Brain dashboard launchers, but they no longer render as full Skills-tab notices. The tab still preserves Brain /skills/index route/profile/generated metadata, checks for the current catalog fields (domain facets, tag facets, reuse groups, and proposal summary), reads Brain /fleet-report for Fleet live/partial/cache/stale-contract status, reads Brain /graph/app/data for Graph neighbor/source-authority contract status, and opens Brain Fleet, Health, Skills, Learning, Agents, or Graph through a fixed main-process whitelist instead of a free-form URL. Launcher buttons inherit the active IDACC review state: stale Fleet, Skills, Agents, Graph, Core, or Learning conditions are warning-styled and ask for confirmation with the review reason before opening the Brain tab; Markdown links to known Brain tabs use the same guarded opener, while Brain-side Health and Learning nav links are now read-only and no longer carry mutation-surface confirmations after Brain restart/redeploy. Local create, plugin digestion, delete, direct skill attach/remove, and manual re-categorize actions refresh the live catalog/roster path and mark Brain skill sync pending where the local catalog changed; if Brain reports fewer skills than the local catalog, the same skill-sync strip appears without automatically mutating Brain. If Brain returns counts but is missing the searchable catalog contract fields, Preview & sync Brain is disabled until Brain is restarted/redeployed with the current /skills/index contract, and the detail stays in the button tooltip plus guarded Brain Skills launcher. Preview & sync Brain fresh-reads safe Brain /health, verifies the current /skills/index route/profile/facet/reuse/no-store contract, then fresh-reads local SKILL.md entries, auto-tag overlays, and Brain summary before showing the additive upsert/shared-memory boundary; it rechecks both the local catalog and the Brain /skills/index summary after confirmation before sending the catalog stamp, so a Brain-side refresh during review blocks the write. The sync additively upserts local library skills into Brain's /skills/index graph and writes a shared catalog memory, but it does not delete Brain-only skill nodes or change which agents have a skill installed. Deleting a local skill therefore refreshes the shared catalog memory but intentionally leaves Brain-learned or previously synced nodes for graph review after the operator previews and applies sync. The Brain fleet checks are read-only: when /fleet-report is unavailable, cache/partial, warning-bearing, missing the current authority-label contract, or reporting counts that disagree with IDACC's live all-team store, Capabilities keeps that state inside guarded Brain Fleet, Health, or Agents launchers instead of the Skills catalog. A mismatch turns the Brain Fleet launcher review into a drift state with both counts, so stale Brain dashboards such as 19/38 beside IDACC 0/67 cannot look authoritative; the nested Brain source now also marks fallback:ID_TEAM snapshots as partial when manager /teams is unavailable. The Brain graph check follows the same pattern for /graph/app/data: it checks that filtered Graph search honors neighbors=0 and that source-authority labels are present before operators trust Graph agent/entity status copy.

## Scope note

Team scoping happens inside this page rather than only through the global footer selector. Keep interop fields explicit: MCP, OpenAPI, A2A, and signed-write discovery are separate concerns and should not be collapsed into one generic capability claim.
